text: 1 | P a g e
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
REPUBLIC OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal made under
Section 331 of the Code of Criminal
Procedure Act No.15 of 1979 read with
Article 138(1) of the Constitution of the
Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri
Lanka.
Court of Appeal Case No. 1. Marakkala Manage Sampath
CA/HCC/ 0188-0189/2023 Priyankara
High Court of Colombo 2. Jayaweera Arachchige Don Deshapriya
Case No. HCB/1961/2013 Ranasinghe
Accused-Appellants
Vs.
The Director General
Commission to Investigate
Allegations of Bribery or Corruption
No.36, Malalasekera Mawatha,
Colomb0-07.
Complainant-Respondent
2 | P a g e
BEFORE : P. Kumararatnam, J.
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
COUNSEL : Anil Silva, PC with Anjana Abeyratne for
the 1st Appellant.
A.S.M. Perera, PC with Janani De Silva for
the 2nd Appellant.
Asitha Antony, Deputy Director General,
Bribery Commission for the Respondent.
ARGUED ON : 19/01/2026
DECIDED ON : 24/03/2026
JUDGMENT
P. Kumararatnam, J.
The above-named Accused-Appellants (hereinafter referred to as the
Appellants) were indicted by the Director General of the Bribery Commission
in the High Court of Colombo.
1. Between 01st August 2011 and 21st September 2011 at Bandarawela,
which is within the jurisdiction of this Court, the 1st accused being a
public servant, to wit, a Member of the Municipal Council of
Bandarawela did solicit a gratification of a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/-
from Sawkenda Mudiyanselage Piyasena as an inducement or reward
3 | P a g e
to do an official act, to wit, to take steps to handover a Boutique at the
Vegetable Wholesale Market situated within the area of the
Bandarawela Municipal Council and thereby had committed an
offence punishable under Section 19(b) of the Bribery Act.
2. At the same time and place and in the course of the same transaction
referred to in Charge 1, the 1st accused being a public servant, to wit,
a Member of the Municipal Council of Bandarawela did solicit a
gratification of a sum of Rs. 2,000,000/- from Sawkenda
Mudiyanselage Piyasena and thereby committed an offence punishable
under Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act as amended.
3. On 22nd September 2011 at the same place and in the course of the
same transaction referred to in Charge 1, the 1st accused being a
public servant, to wit, a Member of the Municipal Council of
Bandarawela did accept a gratification of a sum of Rs. 800,000/- from
Sawkenda Mudiyanselage Piyasena as an inducement or reward to do
an official act, to wit, to take steps to handover a Boutique at the
Vegetable Wholesale Market situated within the area of the
Bandarawela Municipal Council and thereby committed an offence
punishable under Section 19(b) of the Bribery Act.
4. At the same time and place and in the course of the same transaction
referred to in Charge 3, the 1st accused being a public servant, to wit,
a Member of the Municipal Council of Bandarawela did accept a
gratification of a sum of Rs. 800,000/- from Sawkenda Mudiyanselage
Piyasena and thereby committed an offence punishable under Section
19(c) of the Bribery Act as amended.
5. At the same time and place and in the course of the same transaction
referred to in Charge 3, the 2nd accused had abetted the 1st accused to
commit the offence referred to in charge 3 and as a consequence of the
said abatement, the 1st accused had committed the said offence and
thereby the 2nd accused committed an offence punishable under
4 | P a g e
Section 19(b) of the Bribery Act read with Section 25(2) of the Bribery
Act.
6. At the same time and place and in the course of the same transaction
referred to in Charge 4, the 2nd accused had abetted the 1st accused to
commit the offence referred to in charge 4 and as a consequence of the
said abatement the 1st accused had committed the said offence and
thereby the 2nd accused committed an offence punishable under
Section 19(c) of the Bribery Act read with Section 25(2) of the Bribery
Act as amended.
The prosecution had called 09 witnesses and marked documents P1 to P7
and closed their case. Having been satisfied that there is a case to be
answered by the Appellants, the learned High Court Judge had called for the
defence. Both Appellants had given evidence from the witness box and had
marked productions V1 to V7 and closed their case.
The learned High Court Judge by his judgment dated 28.10.2022 found the
1st Appellant guilty of Count 2 and 4 and had acquitted the 1st Appellant
from Counts 1 and 3.
The learned High Court Judge found the 2nd Appellant guilty of Count
number 6 and had acquitted him from Count number 5.
The learned Judge of the High Court of Colombo had imposed the following
sentences on the Appellants.
For the 2nd Count the 1st Appellant was sentenced to 05 years rigorous
imprisonment with a fine of Rs.5,000/- subject to a default sentence of 01-
year simple imprisonment.
For the 4th Count the 1st Appellant was sentenced to 05 years rigorous
imprisonment with a fine of Rs.5,000/- subject to a default sentence of 01-
year simple imprisonment. Additionally, the 1st Appellant was imposed a fine
of Rs.800,000/- with a default sentence of 01-year simple imprisonment.
5 | P a g e
The Court further ordered that the jail sentences imposed on Counts 2-4
against the 1st Appellant to run consecutively.
For the 6th Count the 2nd Appellant was sentenced to 05 years rigorous
imprisonment with a fine of Rs.5,000/- subject to a default sentence of 01-
year simple imprisonment.
Being aggrieved by the aforesaid convictions and sentences, the Appellants
preferred this appeal to this court.
The Appellants were present in this Court on the day of the argument as they
were on bail pending appeal granted by the High Court.
Background of the case albeit briefly is as follows:
According to PW1, Piyasena had been a businessman in the Bandarawela
Town but had handed over his shop to another person. The Bandarawela
Municipal Council had demolished this shop in order to put up a shopping
complex. As the shops which were constructed by the Bandarawela
Municipal Council were going to be rented out, PW1 also tried to get one unit
from the shopping complex. When he met the Mayor of the Bandarawela
Municipal Council, he was informed that all the shops in the shopping
complex had already been allocated.
Having come to know that he could secure a shop through the 1st Appellant
who was a member of the Bandarawela Municipal Council, he had met the
1st Appellant at the Municipality. The 1st Appellant had then solicited 2.2
million Rupees from him to secure a unit in the shopping complex. The 1st
Appellant had then told PW1 that he had to give one million Rupees to the
Mayor, and that the balance would be shared among the Appellants.
PW1 had lodged a complaint with the Bribery Commission and a trap was
arranged. According to the prosecution, the 1st Appellant had accepted
Rs.800,000/- from the complainant.
6 | P a g e
Both Appellants had given evidence from the witness box. According to the
1st Appellant, as the Complainant wanted a shop, he had introduced a person
called Daya Punchihewa, a businessman who wanted to lease out his shop
to another person. To enter into an agreement, the complainant and Daya
Punchihewa had been taken to the office of Ruwan Basnayake, Attorney -at-
Law. As the documents were not prepared, the Complainant was asked to
come on the following day with Rs.800,000/- in cash and three blank
cheques to be given to Daya Punchihewa. On the following day, the 1st
Appellant was taken to a three-wheeler which was parked close to the
Bandarawela Municipal Council, where the Appellant alleges that
Rs.800,000/- was forcibly given to him and that he was arrested.
Subsequently, the 2nd Appellant too was arrested.
On behalf of the 1st Appellant, the following Grounds of Appeal were raised:
1. Has the prosecution proved the case beyond a reasonable doubt?
2. Has the Appellant’s evidence been rejected without reasonable
grounds?
3. Have the matters favourable to the Appellant not been evaluated
properly by the learned High Court Judge?
The Learned President’s Counsel for the 2nd Appellant informed the court
that he too sails with the appeal grounds put forward by the 1st Appellant.
As all the grounds of appeal raised by the Learned President’s Counsel for
the 1st Appellant are interconnected, I will hereby consider all the grounds of
appeal together as follows.
In a criminal trial, it is incumbent on the prosecution to prove the case
beyond a reasonable doubt. There is no burden on the Appellant to prove his
innocence. This is the “Golden Thread” as discussed in Woolmington v. DPP
[1935] A.C.462. In this case Viscount Sankey J held that:
“Throughout the web of the English Criminal Law one golden thread is
always to be seen, that it is the duty of the prosecution to prove the
7 | P a g e
prisoner’s guilt…… If, at the end of and on the whole of the case, there
is a reasonable doubt, created by the evidence given by either the
prosecution or the prisoner…..the prosecution has not made out the case
and the prisoner is entitled to an acquittal.”
In the case of Shivaji Sahebrao Bobade & Anr. v. State of Maharashtra
AIR 1973 SC 2622, the Supreme Court of India held that:
“the cherished principles of the golden thread of proof beyond
reasonable doubt which runs through the web of our law should not be
stretched morbidly to embrace every hunch and degree of doubt. … Only
reasonable doubts belong to the accused. Otherwise, any practical
system of justice will break down and lose credibility with the
community.”
At the very outset, the learned President’s Counsel for the 2nd Appellant
informed the Court that he would agree with the argument put forward by
the Learned President’s Counsel for the 1st Appellant.
Each charge delves on the basis that the 1st Appellant had solicited and
accepted a bribe from the complainant, PW1 in order to secure a shop for
him in the vegetable whole sale market. The 2nd Appellant was charged for
aiding and abetting the 1st Appellant to accept the money from the
Complainant.
As stated earlier the position held by the 1st Appellant was that he had only
facilitated the Complainant to purchase a shop belonging to one Daya
Punchihewa.
According to PW1, Piyasena had gone to the Ornamental Fish Shop belonging
to the 1st Appellant’s father along with two Bribery Officers on 22.09.2011.
There a discussion had taken place, and the 1st Appellant had then left. At
that time, no solicitation was made or any gratification was accepted.
8 | P a g e
An Investigation Officer of CIABOC was selected as the decoy, who is PW2 in
this case. Notably, in his evidence, he did not mention about the presence of
two bribery officers at the Ornamental Fish Shop.
After waiting for some time, as the 1st Appellant had not come, they had left
the place. After a short while, PW1 had received a call from the 1st Appellant
requesting him to come to the Bandarawela Urban Council Office. There they
met the 2nd Appellant who had allegedly asked them to give the money to the
1st Appellant.
According to the evidence of PW2 the decoy, after receiving a call the
Complainant and the 1st Appellant had gone to the office of Attorney-at-Law
Ruwan Kumara Basnayake, who is PW08 in this case. The decoy was asked
not to proceed to the attorney’s office by the investigation officer. As such,
he was unaware as to what had taken place there. After returning from the
attorney’s office with the 1st Appellant, the Complainant had told the decoy
that they could not finalise the transaction. As such, the 1st Appellant had
told the Complainant to come on the following day during the evening, with
Rs.800,000/- cash and three blank cheques. As per the request by the decoy,
the parties agreed to come in the morning. Although the money was brought
on that day, no acceptance had taken place.

PW8, the Attorney-at-Law, in his evidence confirmed that the Complainant
had come to his office with a person called Dayananda to transfer a shop
owned by Dayananda to the Complainant. Further, he confirmed the
presence of the 1st Appellant at that occasion.

The denial that the Complainant did not go to the attorney’s office contradicts
the evidence given by PW2, the decoy and PW8, the Attorney-at-Law which
certainly affects the credibility of the witness.
The President’s Counsel highlighted another glaring contradiction between
the evidence of PW1 and PW2. According to PW1, the money which was
allegedly given to the 1st Appellant, was handed over to him by a bribery
officer when they were seated in the three-wheeler.
13 | P a g e
The relevant portion is re-produced below:
Page 343 of the brief.
m% ( oeka uy;auhd wr Wfoa yïn jqkd lsõjdfka" i,a,s fokak lsh,d lsõjd" i,a,s ÿkakdo@

lsh,d iïm;a uka;%S;=udg' .ek,d uu;a yika uy;a;hd;a" wr uy;a;hdg ÿkakd'
But PW2, the decoy, took up the position that the said money was with him
until the conclusion of the raid.
The relevant portion is re-produced below:
Page 416 of the brief.
m% ( ta uqo,a l¿ nE.a tllg odf.k ld Ndrfha o ;snqfKa @

The contradiction highlighted by the defence too will also, without a doubt,
affect the credibility of the prosecution witnesses and certainly strengthen
the version taken up by the defence in this case.
In a bribery case, the factor that determines whether the prosecution will
succeed or fail is the credibility of the complainant. As bribery would mostly
entail a consensual, secretive act which would not involve any direct physical
evidence, the case would depend on the credibility of the witness’s word as
opposed to the accused. In cases with non-credible complainants, it would
be difficult to establish the necessary chain of evidence, therefore this would
adversely affect the possibility of securing a conviction.
14 | P a g e
In Kalinga Padmatilaka v The Director General of Commission to
Investigate Allegation of Bribery or Corruption SC Appeal 99/2007
decided on 30.07.2009 the Supreme Court held that:
“It has to be stressed here that credibility of prosecution witnesses
should be subject to judicial evaluation in totality and not isolated
scrutiny by the Judge. When witnesses make inconsistent statements
in their evidence either at one stage or at 2 stages, the testimony of such
witnesses is unreliable and in the absence of special circumstances, no
conviction can be based on the testimony of such witnesses. On the
other hand, one cannot be unmindful of the proposition that Court cannot
mechanically reject the evidence of any witness.
In Gurcharan Singh V. State of Haryana - Criminal Law Journal 1994 (2)
171 the Court held that:
“Where the material witnesses make inconsistent statements in their
evidence on material particulars, the evidence of such witnesses
becomes unreliable and unworthy of credence, thus making the
prosecution case highly doubtful.”
In the case of State of Uttar Pradesh v. M. K. Anthony [AIR 1985 SC 48]
the court held that:
“While appreciating the evidence of a witness, the approach must be
whether the evidence of the witness read as a whole appears to have a
ring of truth. Once that impression is formed, it is undoubtedly
necessary for the court to scrutinise the evidence more particularly
keeping in view the deficiencies, draw-backs and infirmities pointed out
in the evidence as a whole and evaluate them to find out whether it is
against the general tenor of the evidence given by the witness and
whether the earlier evaluation of the evidence is shaken as to render it
unworthy of belief. Minor discrepancies on trivial matters not touching
the core of the case, hyper-technical approach by taking sentences torn
15 | P a g e
out of context here or there from the evidence, attaching importance to
some technical error committed by the investigating officer not going to
the: root of the matter would not ordinarily permit rejection of the
evidence as a whole..…Even honest and truthful witnesses may differ
in some details unrelated to the main incident because power of
observation, retention and reproduction differ with individuals. Cross
examination is an unequal duel between a rustic and refined lawyer.”
The prosecution had mainly relied on the evidence given by PW1 and PW2 to
prove the charges levelled against the Appellants. However, upon
examination of their evidence, it is clear that PW1 and PW2 are unreliable
witnesses. PW1 had completely suppressed the fact about going to the
attorney’s office in order to enter into a transfer agreement with a person
called Dayananda who owned a shop in the Bandarawela Municipality
premises. Unfortunately, the said Dayananda was dead before he could give
evidence in this case.
These contradictions are of great importance given the fact that the
Appellants had consistently taken the stance that they had merely facilitated
the Complainant to purchase a shop belonging to Dayananda.
Although the above-mentioned contradictions were not marked, it is the duty
of the Trial Judge to consider such evidence, which is favourable to the
Accused, with utmost care.
In criminal trials, judges bear the responsibility and fundamental duty of
ensuring a fair trial. Doing so requires a careful consideration of all the
evidence available, including exculpatory evidence, which refers to evidence
which would be favourable to the defence. This duty also entails maintaining
the presumption of innocence and ensuring that an accused is not convicted
merely due to a one-sided presentation of the case.
16 | P a g e
Furthermore, it is pertinent to note that right to a fair trial that every accused
has. As such, according to the principles of a fair trial, the accused’s
innocence or guilt in respect of the alleged crime will be ascertained by way
of the legal procedure set in place to guarantee the fair and just treatment of
individuals accused of crimes. This includes the right to a hearing by an
independent and impartial court within a reasonable time period, the
presumption of innocence until proven guilty, the right to legal
representation, the ability to present one’s case and the right to question and
cross examine evidence against them. While this right is an internationally
recognised and respected human right, they act as a major cornerstone of
democracy, due to their role in ensuring fairness and justice and to limit
abuse of powers of governments and other state authorities and institutions.
In the case of Rowe and Davis v the United Kingdom [2000] ECHR, The
European Court of Human Rights held:
“… in a criminal case, that both prosecution and defence must be given the
opportunity to have knowledge of and comment on the observations filed
and the evidence adduced by the other party.”
The Supreme Court in The Queen v Liyanage and Others (1963) 65 NLR
337, stated that:
“… on a trial upon Indictment, by the very essence of the pre-trial
procedure, an accused person becomes aware of all the evidence relied
upon by the Crown in support of the Indictment and which the Crown
intends to place against him at the trial. He is entitled in law to know such
evidence before he is called upon to plead to the Indictment. It is a right of
his, inherent in that procedure.”
In this case with the glaring contradictions, the learned High Court Judge
has come to his own conclusion that the 2nd ,4th and 6th charges had been
proved beyond a reasonable doubt.
17 | P a g e
The Apex Court of Sri Lanka has established precedent in a number of cases
stating that the defense would prevail when there is a contradiction that
affects the core of the case.
When a witness gives evidence before the court upon oath, he or she is
expected to tell the truth in a court of law. If the person gives contradictory
evidence opposite to the statement, it is usually called a contradiction. It is
the duty of the trial judge to unearth the truth and to arrive at a conclusion
on whether an Accused person is guilty or not.
In the case of State Rep. by Inspector of Police v. Saravanan AIR 2009 SC
152 the Court held that:
“The contradictions/omissions must be of such nature which materially
affects the trial. Minor contradictions, inconsistencies, embellishments
or improvements which do not affect the core of the prosecution case
should not be made a ground to reject the evidence of the witness in its
entirety.”
In the case of Shamal Ghosh Vs. State of West Bengal 2012 All. S. C. R,
1921the Court held that:
“Omission to state a fact whether it is a material contradiction or not is
a question of fact. The discretion is left with the Court to determine
whether it is a contradiction or material contradiction which renders the
entire evidence of the witness untrustworthy and affects the case of
prosecution materially.
In the recent Court of Appeal judgment CA HCC 190/2024, dated
28.03.2025 it was noted that:
“It is essential to recognise that while discrepancies may exist, they do not
necessarily reflect a lack of credibility and reliability; instead, it is quite
clear that they stem from the complexities of human memory or the detailed
18 | P a g e
nature of the events in question. Ultimately, these issues should not
distract from the comprehensive truth the prosecution seems to establish.
It is essential to understand the implications of such discrepancies and
the broader principles of justice that must prevail. Therefore, absence of a
precise date should not be viewed as a lack of credibility rather it
highlights the need for sensitivity and understanding the judicial process.
Moreover, the cornerstone of a fair trial lies in the comprehensive
examination of all evidence presented, rather than singular date. To
dismiss a case solely due to an imprecise date undermines the principal of
justice and the potential for truth to be revealed through crucial
examination.”
In the case of Mohamed Niyas Naufer and others v. Attorney General (Sc.
01/2006 decided on 08/12/2006), the Court observed that;
"when faced with contradictions in a witness's testimonial, the Court must
bear in mind the nature and significance of the contradictions, viewed in
light of the whole of the evidence given by the witness."
As the contradictory evidence given by PW1 and PW2 certainly affects the
root of the prosecution’s case, I conclude that the prosecution has not proved
the case against the Appellants beyond a reasonable doubt. Therefore, the
grounds urged by the Learned President’s Counsel for the 1st Appellant have
merit.
The Learned President’s Counsel who appeared for the 2nd Appellant while
endorsing the argument put forward by the learned President’s Counsel on
behalf of the 1st Appellant, strenuously argued that the charges against the
2nd Appellant in the indictment had not been proved beyond a reasonable
doubt by the prosecution. As such, the 2nd Appellant cannot be charged for
aiding and abetting the 1st Appellant in committing the alleged crime.
19 | P a g e
As the prosecution has failed to prove the charges beyond a reasonable doubt
against the Appellants, I allow the appeal and acquit them from all the
charges.
The Appeal is allowed.
The Registrar is directed to send this judgment to the High Court of Colombo
along with the original case record.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
R. P. Hettiarachchi, J.
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
